title: Property Registration Process in Bangladesh 2026: Complete Step-by-Step Guide
metaDescription: Complete guide to property registration in Bangladesh 2026. Learn steps, documents, stamp duty, fees, mutation process, and how to avoid registration fraud.
heroIntro: Registering property in Bangladesh is a mandatory legal step that protects your ownership rights. An unregistered sale deed is not admissible as evidence of ownership in court. This complete 2026 guide covers the entire property registration process — from preparing documents to paying stamp duty, registration at the Sub-Registrar's office, and completing mutation at the AC Land office.

quickAnswer.heading: ⚡ Quick Answer: Property Registration Bangladesh
points: Property registration is mandatory under the Registration Act 1908 for all immovable property transfers
Pay stamp duty (based on property value) and registration fee at the Sub-Registrar's office
Required documents: deed, NID, khatian, previous deed, tax clearance, photographs
After registration, apply for mutation (namjari) at the AC Land office to update government records
Use a land lawyer to verify title and check for encumbrances before purchase

toc: Why Property Registration Is Mandatory in Bangladesh
Step 1: Due Diligence and Title Verification
Step 3: Calculating and Paying Stamp Duty & Registration Fees
Step 4: Registration at the Sub-Registrar's Office
Step 5: Mutation (Namjari) at the AC Land Office
Common Property Registration Frauds and How to Avoid Them

sections.h2: Why Property Registration Is Mandatory in Bangladesh
sections.content:
Under the Registration Act 1908 (Section 17), any transfer of immovable property worth more than BDT 100 must be registered. For practical purposes, all property transactions of value must be registered. The consequences of failing to register include:

  • An unregistered deed cannot be used as evidence of ownership in any court of law
  • The transfer of ownership is legally ineffective against third parties
  • The buyer is vulnerable to the seller transferring the same property to another buyer who registers first
  • Difficulty in obtaining bank loans or mortgages against unregistered property
  • Problems passing the property to heirs or future buyers

Registration creates a public record of ownership that protects buyers. Always complete registration promptly after purchase. For assistance with title verification and registration, consult our property law specialists.

sections.h2: Step 1: Due Diligence and Title Verification
sections.content:
Before registering a property, conduct thorough due diligence to ensure the seller has a clear title and the property is free from disputes or encumbrances:

  1. Obtain certified copies of khatians: CS, SA, and RS khatians from the District Record Room or AC Land office — these confirm the land's survey history and rightful owner
  2. Verify the deed chain: Obtain certified copies of all previous registered deeds for the property from the Sub-Registrar's office. Trace ownership back at least 25–30 years.
  3. Check for encumbrances: Search the Sub-Registrar's record for any mortgage (bandez), charge, or other encumbrance on the property
  4. Verify mutation records: Check whether the seller's name appears in the mutation (namjari) records at the AC Land office
  5. Inspect the physical property: Visit the property to verify boundaries match the documents and confirm there are no adverse possessors or encroachers
  6. Check for pending litigation: Ask the seller for a disclosure of any pending cases involving the property

Hiring a qualified land lawyer to conduct due diligence is strongly recommended before committing to a property purchase.

The sale deed (dastabon) is the primary legal document that transfers ownership. It must be prepared carefully to include:

  • Full details of both parties: Buyer and seller names, father's names, addresses, and National ID numbers
  • Complete property description: CS/SA/RS dag numbers, khatian numbers, mouza name, upazila, district, total area, and boundaries (north, south, east, west)
  • Purchase price: The agreed consideration amount (must be accurate — under-declaring price is a common but illegal practice)
  • Previous deed references: Reference to the seller's own title deed(s)
  • Warranty clause: The seller's warranty that the property is free from encumbrances and they have full authority to sell
  • Witness details: At least two witnesses are required

The deed must be typed/printed on appropriate stamp paper. Your lawyer will draft the deed to ensure legal accuracy and compliance with current registration requirements. Errors in the deed description can cause serious problems in future transfers or legal proceedings.

sections.h2: Step 3: Calculating and Paying Stamp Duty & Registration Fees
sections.content:
Stamp duty and registration fees are significant costs in Bangladesh property transactions. As of 2026:

Stamp Duty: Calculated on the higher of the stated consideration price or the government-published upazila/district market value (mouza rate). Typical rates range from 3% to 4% of property value depending on the area.

Registration Fee: Typically 1% of the property value.

Local Government Tax: An additional local government tax (Dhaka City Corporation area typically 2%) applies in many urban areas.

Gain Tax (Capital Gains Tax): On the seller's side, a source tax on property transfer income applies. The rate depends on the seller's category (individual, company) and property location.

How to Pay:

  1. Stamp duty is paid by purchasing stamp paper (non-judicial) from authorized stamp vendors or through the e-Porcha/online payment system
  2. Registration fee is paid at the Sub-Registrar's office treasury or by bank draft
  3. Obtain payment receipts — these are essential for the registration appointment

Total transaction costs (stamp duty + registration + taxes) typically range from 7% to 12% of property value in Bangladesh. Budget for this significant additional cost when purchasing property.

sections.h2: Step 4: Registration at the Sub-Registrar's Office
sections.content:
Property registration in Bangladesh is completed at the Sub-Registrar's office having jurisdiction over the location of the property. Here is the process:

  1. Book an Appointment: Visit or contact the relevant Sub-Registrar's office to schedule a registration appointment. Some offices now accept applications online or through the District e-Service center.
  2. Attend with All Parties: Both buyer(s) and seller(s) must be physically present at the Sub-Registrar's office on the registration date, along with two witnesses.
  3. Submit Documents: Present the prepared deed, stamp papers, payment receipts, national ID copies of all parties, photographs, khatian copies, and previous deed copies.
  4. Sub-Registrar Verification: The Sub-Registrar examines documents, verifies identities, and records thumb impressions/signatures of all parties and witnesses.
  5. Registration Completion: The deed is officially registered and given a unique book number, volume number, and serial number in the register. This number is your registration reference.
  6. Collect Registered Deed: After registration (which may take a few days to process), collect the registered original deed from the Sub-Registrar's office. The deed is now an official government record.

sections.h2: Step 5: Mutation (Namjari) at the AC Land Office
sections.content:
After registration of the deed, the next critical step is mutation (namjari) — updating the government land records to reflect the new owner's name. Without mutation, the government records still show the previous owner, creating complications for future sales, inheritance, and tax payments.

Where to Apply: The AC Land (Assistant Commissioner Land) office at the Upazila level having jurisdiction over the property location.

Documents Required for Mutation:

  • Certified copy of the registered deed
  • CS/SA/RS khatian copies
  • National ID of the buyer
  • Application form (available at AC Land office)
  • Court fee stamp (nominal amount)
  • Land development tax payment receipt (up-to-date)

Process: After filing the mutation application, the AC Land office issues a notice to neighboring landholders. If no objection is received, the mutation is approved and the land records are updated. The entire process takes approximately 30–90 days.

Importance of Mutation: Mutated land records allow you to pay land development tax (khajna) in your own name and use the mutation certificate as additional evidence of ownership in any future legal proceedings.

sections.h2: Common Property Registration Frauds and How to Avoid Them
sections.content:
Property fraud is common in Bangladesh. Protect yourself by knowing these warning signs:

  • Selling land without title: The seller has no registered deed tracing back to a khatian — verify the complete deed chain before purchase
  • Forged Power of Attorney: A fraudster sells property using a fabricated power of attorney from the real owner — always verify POA authenticity directly with the original owner
  • Multiple sale of same property: The seller sells the same land to multiple buyers — check Sub-Registrar records for recent transactions involving the same property
  • Under-declared price: Parties declare a lower price to reduce stamp duty — this is illegal and creates risks if the transaction is ever challenged in court
  • Encumbered property: The seller has a mortgage or pending case on the property but conceals it — always conduct a thorough encumbrance search at the Sub-Registrar's office
  • Fake khatians: Fraudulent or altered khatian documents — verify all khatians against original records at the District Record Room

Working with an experienced land lawyer in Bangladesh for due diligence before any property purchase is the most effective protection against these frauds. Read our guide on handling land disputes in Bangladesh if you encounter problems after purchase.

faqs.question: How long does property registration take in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: The actual registration appointment at the Sub-Registrar's office typically takes a few hours. However, receiving the registered deed (after processing) can take 3–10 working days. The subsequent mutation process at the AC Land office takes approximately 30–90 days.

faqs.question: What is the stamp duty rate for property in Dhaka, Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Stamp duty in Dhaka varies by area and property type. For residential property in Dhaka City Corporation, total transfer costs (stamp duty, registration fee, local government tax, and capital gains tax) typically amount to 7–12% of the transaction value. Confirm current rates with a land lawyer as rates may be updated by the national budget.

faqs.question: Can I register property online in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Bangladesh has been gradually introducing digital registration processes. Some districts offer online appointment booking and pre-submission of documents. However, physical attendance at the Sub-Registrar's office by both buyer and seller is still required for final deed execution and thumb impression verification.

faqs.question: What happens if I buy property that is not registered in my name?
faqs.answer: Without registration, your title is not legally effective against third parties. An unregistered deed cannot be used as evidence of ownership in court. If the seller later sells the same property to another buyer who registers it, that buyer may have superior legal rights. Always register immediately after purchase.

faqs.question: Is mutation (namjari) the same as registration?
faqs.answer: No. Registration at the Sub-Registrar's office creates the legal transfer of title between buyer and seller. Mutation (namjari) at the AC Land office updates the government land records to reflect the new owner's name. Both steps are necessary — registration establishes the legal transfer, while mutation updates the administrative record used for tax purposes.
